## Dr. Elias Vance stood before the humming machine.

Dr. Elias Vance stared at the gauges on his lab wall as the countdown ticked away. For months, he had been trying to reverse entropy, to grasp the slippery fabric of time and pull it back against its relentless tide. His fingers twitched slightly on the keyboard, his heart pounding a rhythm that matched the oscillating hum of the temporal device beneath the floorboards. This wasn’t just about science; it was about legacy. Every second delayed meant less progress toward the cure he sought. Less time meant more suffering for the patients waiting outside the clinic.

He adjusted the dials with trembling precision, focusing intensely on the blue light that flickered ominously on the console. The readings stabilized, showing a fraction of a second passing instead of the full duration that the universe demanded. He took a breath and stepped forward. “Time,” he whispered, his voice echoing in the silent room. The air grew thick, heavier than usual. The coffee mug sat firmly on the desk, but the steam rising from it seemed to hover longer than physics allowed.

The world slowed, and then stopped, then ran backward in Elias’s eyes. He watched a leaf drift down from the window. It paused in mid-air. It hung suspended against the grey sky. He reached out, but his hand passed through the space where the leaf used to be. The object moved backward toward the branch, defying gravity and causality. He felt a surge of adrenaline, followed by fear, knowing that whatever he had discovered could never truly be undone.

## Dr. Elias Vance stood before the humming machine.

Dr. Elias Vance stared at the gauges on his lab wall as the countdown ticked away. For months, he had been trying to reverse entropy, to grasp the slippery fabric of time and pull it back against its relentless tide. His fingers twitched slightly on the keyboard, his heart pounding a rhythm that matched the oscillating hum of the temporal device beneath the floorboards. This wasn’t just about science; it was about legacy. Every second delayed meant less progress toward the cure he sought. Less time meant more suffering for the patients waiting outside the clinic.

He adjusted the dials with trembling precision, focusing intensely on the blue light that flickered ominously on the console. The readings stabilized, showing a fraction of a second passing instead of the full duration that the universe demanded. He took a breath and stepped forward. “Time,” he whispered, his voice echoing in the silent room. The air grew thick, heavier than usual. The coffee mug sat firmly on the desk, but the steam rising from it seemed to hover longer than physics allowed.

The world slowed, and then stopped, then ran backward in Elias’s eyes. He watched a leaf drift down from the window. It paused in mid-air. It hung suspended against the grey sky. He reached out, but his hand passed through the space where the leaf used to be. The object moved backward toward the branch, defying gravity and causality. He felt a surge of adrenaline, followed by fear, knowing that whatever he had discovered could never truly be undone.

Elias pulled back from the console, his legs shaking under his weight. He knew the implications were catastrophic. If everyone experienced time at different speeds, society would crumble into chaos. He grabbed the manual and shoved it deep into his pockets. The machine hummed lower, settling into silence. He left the room, locking the door behind him, leaving the future to its own devices.
